text.title: Hybrid Delivery — Patterns, Decision Framework, and Boundary Rules
text.summary: Five named hybrid patterns with their applicability, a decision framework for choosing agile vs. predictive vs. hybrid, and the boundary and translation-layer rules required for coherent reporting.

list.item: Water-Scrum-Fall: fixed requirements (predictive) → iterative development (Scrum) → predictive deployment and ops. Use for hardware + software products.
Agile with Gates: Scrum sprints run inside stage-gate approval checkpoints. Use for regulated industries (FDA, FAA, SOX).
Phased Agile: large programs where multiple teams iterate within phases governed by predictive milestones. Use for enterprise rollouts.
Selective Agile: some components use agile (frontend, user research), others stay predictive (backend stable, compliance). Use where components have different risk profiles.
DevOps + Agile: continuous delivery on the agile arm with quarterly or annual governance checkpoints. The 2026 winning pattern for cloud platforms.

rule.statement: Select the delivery approach by component risk profile, not by organizational preference or trend. High uncertainty + frequent change → agile; clear requirements + fixed scope → predictive; mixed characteristics → hybrid; continuous deployment needed → agile + DevOps.
rule.rationale: Method dogma ("we do agile here") applied without fit analysis produces cargo-cult implementations. The framework must reflect actual project characteristics, not team culture preferences.

rule.statement: Draw the boundary in writing inside a program charter or program.yaml: name explicitly which deliverables are predictive (contracts, regulatory submissions, hardware BOMs, charters) and which are agile (software increments, user research, UX iterations).
rule.rationale: Boundary ambiguity is the dominant hybrid failure mode. An undocumented boundary turns into a status theatre where teams argue about which method applies to any given decision.

rule.statement: Define exactly one translation table per program: epic ↔ deliverable, story-points ↔ effort, velocity ↔ run rate. Use it for every steering report; never invent new mappings mid-program.
rule.rationale: Without a fixed translation table, steering-committee reporting is inconsistent across presentations. Finance, PMO, and dev teams read the same data with incompatible vocabulary, leading to false alignment.

rule.statement: Use at most two tools with explicit sync; three or more tools is a smell requiring immediate consolidation.
rule.rationale: Each additional tool adds a sync gap. Agents waste tokens reconciling disagreements between tools; humans lose trust in all data sources when tools disagree.

rule.statement: Match gate frequency to risk profile: monthly for regulated programs, quarterly for steady-state, never weekly (weekly gates are status meetings with a governance label).
rule.rationale: Over-gating the agile arm throttles delivery frequency without commensurate risk reduction. Under-gating the predictive arm creates unbounded risk accumulation. Calibrate gates to actual decision needs.

text.reference: Script that flags milestone/epic misalignment from a program.yaml file.
